sentence: Subjective:
HPI: The patient presents with a 2-3 week history of right shoulder pain, initially developing after a few days of strenuous yard work.
The pain is located on the outside of the shoulder, more tender when raising the arm, and is exacerbated by movement.
The patient reports no restriction in arm movement, but experiences pain when moving the arm.
The pain is intermittent and not constant, with occasional nocturnal exacerbation due to sleeping on the affected side.
The patient has taken Advil and applied ice to alleviate the pain, with some relief.
The patient has a history of occasional left shoulder pain from a childhood injury, but this is different in location and character.
Past medical history: The patient has a history of occasional shoulder pain from a childhood injury.
No other past medical conditions or relevant social history mentioned.
Review of systems: The patient denies numbness in the arm or hands.
Current medications: The patient is taking Advil for pain relief.
Objective:
Vital signs: The patient's temperature is 97.8°F, heart rate is 62 beats per minute, respirations are 16 breaths per minute, blood pressure is 116/84 mmHg, and oxygen saturation is 99%.
Physical exam: The patient has limited active and passive range of motion of the right shoulder, with tenderness at the greater tuberosity of the humerus.
The acromioclavicular and sternoclavicular joints are not tender.
The patient has a good hand grip and intact sensation to touch on the right arm.
Neurovascular exam is normal.
Test Results: The patient's shoulder X-ray is negative for fracture.
Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: The patient's symptoms are most likely due to rotator cuff tendinopathy, a condition characterized by injury to the tendons of the muscles that help make the shoulder muscles.
Problem list: Rotator cuff tendinopathy; suspected rotator cuff injury; [NOT MENTIONED]
Plan:
I plan to refer the patient to physical therapy for approximately 6-8 weeks to address the rotator cuff tendinopathy.
I recommend the patient continue taking Tylenol for pain relief and consider a steroid injection if symptoms do not improve.
I will order an MRI to further evaluate the condition.
Follow-up: The patient will follow up with the physical therapist for the prescribed treatment plan.
